data.title: Live results: Mejia, Hathaway duke it out to replace Sherrill in New Jersey House special election
data.description:
Democrat Analilia Mejia is facing off against Republican Joe Hathaway in a Thursday special election to fill the House seat left open by New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill.

Mejia’s surprise primary victory earlier this year marked another win for progressives. She is widely expected to defeat Hathaway on Thursday.

Polls close at 8 p.m. EDt. Follow Decision Desk HQ’s live results here.

data.title: First candidate announces run for Wisconsin high court
data.description:
Clark County Circuit Court Judge Lyndsey Brunette became the first candidate to announce a run for a spot on the Wisconsin Supreme Court — an announcement that came just one week after the Badger State held a contest for another seat on the high court. 

“Today, on the bench, I make sure the law is applied fairly and equally because in our courts, justice shouldn’t depend on who you are, your politics or how much money you have,” Brunette, who was elected in her role as a Democrat, said in her launch ad. “I’m running for the Wisconsin Supreme Court because those principles matter now more than ever.” 

“Judges should follow the facts and the law, respect the Constitution and protect the rights and freedoms of everyone,” she added. “That means protecting your personal health care rights, safeguarding voting rights, supporting public safety and ensuring fairness for all.” 

Conservative Justice Annette Ziegler announced earlier this year that she would not be seeking reelection for her spot on the state’s high court. While Wisconsin Supreme Court races are technically nonpartisan, major candidates are largely backed by state parties.  

Liberals scored a key win last week when Justice-elect Chris Taylor, a liberal, won an open spot on the state Supreme Court against conservative candidate Maria Lazar. The two were vying for the seat vacated by conservative Justice Rebecca Bradley, who also said she would not be seeking reelection after her current term. 

Once Taylor joins the court, liberals will have a 5-2 majority on the bench. If liberals win the open seat next year, it could further stretch their edge to a 6-1 margin.  

Recent years have underscored how rapidly the makeup of the Wisconsin high court has shifted. Liberals flipped the state Supreme Court for the first time in 15 years when Justice Janet Protasiewicz beat former state Supreme Court Justice Dan Kelly in 2023.

data.title: More young men than women say religion is important to them: Gallup
data.description:
Young men are more concerned with religion than women in the same age group, according to new polling data from Gallup released on Thursday. 

The survey found that 42 percent of adult men between 18 and 29 years old indicated that religion is “very important” in their lives during telephone surveys conducted between 2024 and 2025. Only 29 percent of women in this age range said the same.

The numbers among young male U.S. adults constitute a 14-point increase from Gallup’s results between 2022 and 2023, indicating a growing number of religious interest from this demographic.

Additionally, young women used to be more engaged in religion several years ago, according to Gallup data from 2022 to 2023. Thirty-two percent of women and 28 percent of men said religion was “very important” to them that year. 

A significant number of President Trump’s supporters are white Christians. 

According to a Pew Research Center poll released before the 2024 election, 82 percent of white evangelical voters expressed support for Trump over former Vice President Kamala Harris. Additionally, 61 percent of white Catholics supported Trump over Harris. 

Eighty-six percent of Black Protestants and 65 percent of Hispanic Catholics backed the Democratic candidate in 2024. Jewish voters also largely favored Harris, with 65 percent expressing support for the candidate ahead of the general election, according to the Pew survey. 

This support among white Christian voters has held since the 2024 election, with two-thirds of white evangelicals saying Trump defends people with religious beliefs similar to theirs, according to a Pew poll released on Thursday. 

The president recently sparked backlash from Christians on both sides of the aisle after he posted an AI-generated image on Truth Social that appeared to depict him as Jesus. Trump later took down the post, saying he believed it portrayed him as a doctor.

Former GOP Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (Ga.) criticized Trump and those defending the picture in a Thursday post on the social platform X. 

“Trump knows what he is doing,” the former Trump ally wrote. “He knows what he posted. He knows how to manipulate his followers. And he’s not sorry, he never apologized. Instead he lied, and said he was a doctor, which is also absurd.”

Since posting and deleting the original picture, Trump has continued to double down. On Wednesday, he reposted another AI-created photo that depicted him being embraced by Jesus. 

“The Radical Left Lunatics might not like this, but I think it is quite nice!!!” Trump wrote on Truth Social. 

Some Republican strategists have expressed concerns that these posts and his criticism of the leader of the Catholic Church could distract from the issue of affordability that GOP candidates are hoping to convey to voters ahead of the upcoming midterm elections. 

Mick Mulvaney, Trump’s former chief of staff during his first term, said the post will not help Republican turnout at the polls this November. 

“The midterms are all about turnout,” Mulvaney said. “Republicans need Trump voters to show up in November.  I’m not sure any Republicans think the ‘Jesus/doctor’ post thing encourages people to get to vote GOP.”

The new Gallup poll surveyed 145 U.S. women and 295 men between the ages of 18 and 29 from 2024 and 2025. The margin of error ranges from approximately 7 to 10 percentage points for this age group.

data.title: Senate votes to repeal Biden-era wilderness protections in Minnesota, sending bill to Trump's desk
data.description:
The Senate on Thursday voted to repeal Biden-era protections for a contentious wilderness area in Minnesota, sending the question to President Trump’s desk.

The Senate voted 50-49 to overturn a Biden-era move to block mining in an area around Minnesota’s Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness.

President Trump is likely to sign the measure, which has already passed the House, to repeal the protections.

Sens. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) and Susan Collins (R-Maine) voted with Democrats against the measure. Sen. Josh Hawley (R-Mo.) did not vote.

Boundary Waters is a wilderness area that includes a remote forest in northeastern Minnesota. It is known for its water recreation opportunities including boating, hiking and fishing.

The company Twin Metals has sought to mine nearby – and was blocked from doing so by the Biden administration in 2023.

The vote came under the Congressional Review Act (CRA), which allows Congress to cut regulations finalized in the previous 60 legislative days.

The decision to block mining in the area occurred more than 60 legislative days ago, but a press release from House sponsor Rep. Pete Stauber (R-Minn.) says that the action was not properly transmitted to Congress until the Trump administration. 

The move comes after other Republican efforts to use the CRA in novel and controversial ways, including an effort to axe California clean car standards last year.

data.title: Army chief firing hovers over Driscoll hearing: 5 takeaways
data.description:
The recent firing of the Army’s well-respected chief of staff, Gen. Randy George, hovered over the House Appropriations defense subcommittee hearing on Thursday, with lawmakers from both sides grilling Army Secretary Dan Driscoll and the Army’s acting chief of staff, Gen. Christopher LaNeve.

While President Trump’s $1.5 trillion defense budget request, the U.S. war against Iran, the state of munition stockpiles and various Army programs also factored into discussion, members took the opportunity to question the decision of Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth.

Several Republicans on the panel praised George and Driscoll, who, along with LaNeve — Hegseth’s military adviser last year — were also asked about the Army’s push to bolster U.S. military drone capability, Army’s aviation programs and the state of the defense industrial base. 

Here are the takeaways from the hearing: 

Lawmakers praise Gen. Randy George

Lawmakers in both parties heaped praise on George, who was removed in early April at the request of Hegseth, a decision that stunned many at the Pentagon. Shortly after the ouster was made public, several GOP lawmakers lauded the general as an experienced leader who dedicated his life to the Army. 

“I just want the record to reflect how much we regret — I personally regret, at least — he’s no longer in active service. He’s a real loss to us, in my opinion,” Rep. Tom Cole (R-Okla.), the chair of the House Appropriations Committee, said on Thursday.

Rep. Steve Womack (R-Ark.) said he found George, who spent more than four decades in the Army and was close with Driscoll, to be a “patriotic American.” 

“I won’t go through all of his background, but a distinguished representative of our Army and I, too, regret the fact of the conditions that he left the service, and I think that our country will regret that circumstance; that’s another story,” Womack said. 

High regard for George came from some Democrats, as well. 

Rep. Rosa DeLauro (D-Conn.), ranking member of the House Appropriations Committee, said she was “very upset and disturbed at how summarily” George was dismissed. 

Rep. Ed Case (D-Hawaii) said he had very high confidence in George’s leadership as the U.S. military was actively striking military targets inside Iran. 

“When you collectively — the president, the secretary and yourself — decide to summarily fire the chief of staff of the Army — publicly, overtly, I would even say humiliatingly and cruelly — without any offer, apparently, of a graceful exit, you at least owe Congress, the public, and, I think most importantly, the soldiers some explanation, which you did not do,” Case said. 

Driscoll, when asked about George’s removal, praised the ex-Army chief of staff, but noted that civilian officials “get to pick the leaders that they want.” 

“I was in North Carolina when Gen. George was asked to put in his resignation paperwork, with my family for my kids’ spring break. When we drove back from North Carolina, I drove straight to Gen. George’s house, we walked right in, and we all gave him a hug,” Driscoll told lawmakers. 

“There is no person that has more respect for Gen. George … he was an amazing, transformational leader,” the Army secretary said. “That being said, the civilian leadership, the design of our system, is that they get to pick the leaders that they want, and we execute on those orders.”

Apart from George, two other senior Army officials — Maj. Gen. William Green, the Army’s chief of chaplains and Gen. David Hodne, who was in charge of the Army’s Transformation and Training Command — were removed on April 2, puzzling some lawmakers on the panel. 

“I don’t know what the reasoning is, and I can’t fathom what the reasoning is in letting someone who had, by all accounts, has done a very, very good, capable job in that case,” DeLauro said.

GOP lawmakers back up Driscoll

Republicans showed support for Driscoll, who has been embroiled in a turf war with Hegseth since last year, tensions that have heightened in recent weeks as Trump ousted two Cabinet members and the Pentagon chief has fired dozens of senior military officials. 

“I just want to tell you, I think you are doing an outstanding job. You are the right person in the right place at the right time. I appreciate the energy,” Cole told Driscoll. “I appreciate the practical experience in uniform, as well as what you bring from your experience out of uniform.

Womack, a senior appropriator, said he agreed with Cole’s assessment “about your capability and about your demonstrated leadership in the greatest army on the planet.” 

Rep. Dave Joyce (R-Ohio) applauded Driscoll for his work and “your commitment to developing this next generation of combat vehicles.”  

Concern over munitions stockpiles

As the U.S.-Israeli war in Iran nears its seventh week, a major point of concern is America’s munitions stockpiles, stretched thin in shooting down drones and missiles launched from Iranian territory. 

“We know the administration monitors the level of munitions, but we must come up with a better solution for taking out drones,” said the panel’s ranking member, Rep. Betty McCollum (D-Minn). “Using Patriot missiles that cost $3 million each is not cost effective, and it’s not sustainable.”

She later added: “We need to know — and I know it’s in a classified setting — exactly where the Army is with its munitions. Exactly.”

American forces have struck more than 13,000 targets in Iran since the war began on Feb. 28, in addition to firing countless interceptors at Iranian drones and missiles aimed at U.S. bases, embassies and interests throughout the Persian Gulf.

The Pentagon, looking to quickly increase production of critical munitions, initially asked the White House for $200 billion in supplemental funding for its military campaign. That number is now expected to be between $80 billion and $100 billion.

Cole called the U.S. ability to produce munitions and maintain inventory “urgent.”

“We cannot afford hollow magazines in a high-intensity, protracted conflict. I hope to hear an honest assessment of where we stand today and what investments the Army is making to bolster our readiness, both for the near and long term,” he said.

Later in the hearing, LaNeve said Driscoll is “pushing us really hard” to produce low-cost munitions to be able to help counter drones “and to go after companies that say they can scale and scale fast. And we’re working with them, with our team to do it.” 

Army stands with Ukraine

Driscoll said the U.S. Army has stood with Ukraine since the beginning of the Russian invasion of the country in February 2022 and praised the country’s military for altering the way “humans engage in conflict.” 

“I’ve been to Kyiv. I’ve spent a lot of time with a lot of the Ukrainian leadership,” Driscoll told Rep. Marcy Kaptur (D-Ohio), a staunch ally of Ukraine in Congress, adding that, “I am publicly on record saying we are learning a lot from them, and we are changing to a lot of the lessons that they have taught us.” 

Driscoll’s close ally, Vice President Vance, has often criticized U.S. support for Ukraine, recently said that one of his proudest achievements of the Trump administration is that “we’ve told Europe that if you want to buy weapons, you can, but the United States is not buying weapons and sending them to Ukraine anymore.”

Trump tapped Driscoll last fall to negotiate with Ukrainian officials in hopes of ending the Russia-Ukraine war. 

When Kaptur asked if the administration views Russia as an ally, adversary or undecided, Driscoll said “adversary.” 

Defense budget request

As part of Trump’s $1.5 trillion defense budget for fiscal year 2027, the Army plans to ask for $60.5 billion, including $36 billion in the base budget request and $24 billion in a future reconciliation request, Breaking Defense reported.

But the Army has not yet released justification documents detailing its spending plans, frustrating lawmakers who said they don’t have enough information on the Pentagon’s budget to do their job of oversight. 

“We really do need more information than we have now,” Cole said. “I put that on [Office of Management and Budget].”

McCollum, meanwhile, said for the second year in a row lawmakers have had to hold a hearing “without full details of the Army’s request and that is very disappointing.”

She also called the price tag of Trump’s $1.5 trillion defense budget “shocking”

“To be clear, the size of the request for defense spending is shocking and in the base budget the Department of Defense requests over $200 billion over last year’s enacted levels,” she said. “When combined with mandatory funding for defense requests through the reconciliation bill. That’s an increase of $400 billion.”

data.title: Democrats' fundraising dominates key midterm races: What we learned from the latest campaign filings
data.description:
First-quarter campaign fundraising reports released this week offer new insights on midterm dynamics with the fight for control of Congress in full swing.

Democrats boasted massive hauls in high-stakes House and Senate races over the last three months, according to the latest Federal Election Commission (FEC) filings, pointing to the party’s momentum as primaries begin to solidify November midterm matchups.  

At the same time, Republicans have shown they can be competitive in outside spending, particularly between the two parties’ main super PACs funding Senate races. 

Here are the key takeaways from this year’s first-quarter filings:  

Democrats dominate key Senate races

Democratic Senate candidates dominated fundraising in some of the most closely watched contests.

Democrat James Talarico brought in $27 million in the Texas Senate race, a record-setting Q1 haul for a Senate candidate, as he waits to find out which Republican will emerge from a late-May runoff to face him in November.  

Defending the longtime-red seat, incumbent Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas) brought in $9 million in the first quarter between his campaign and political committees — while Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton’s (R) campaign and joint fundraising committee hauled about $2.2 million, according to The Texas Tribune. 

Georgia Sen. Jon Ossoff, who’s considered one of the most vulnerable Democrat incumbents this cycle, raised roughly $14 million in the first quarter, cementing his advantage over a crowded field of Republican challengers.  

Former North Carolina Gov. Roy Cooper (D) brought in a $13.8 million haul as he races against former Republican National Committee Chair Michael Whatley, who raised $5 million, for an open Senate seat in the Tar Heel State.  

In Ohio, former Sen. Sherrod Brown raised $12.5 million in the first quarter, NOTUS reported, as he prepares his comeback campaign for a likely November face-off with incumbent Sen. Jon Husted (R), who raised $2.9 million in the same period.  

And in Alaska, former Rep. Mary Peltola brought in roughly $9 million in the first quarter, marking the largest first-quarter raised for a Senate candidate in the state as she challenges incumbent Republican Sen. Dan Sullivan, who raised $2.1 million. 

Some top Democrats fell short on fundraising in competitive primaries. For one, Democratic Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner outraised both Gov. Janet Mills (D) and incumbent Sen. Susan Collins (R), though the latter had more cash on hand.  

Still, the hauls in major races are a sign of Democratic momentum and a pocketbook boost for vulnerable campaigns as the party grows bullish about netting the four seats they need to flip the Senate, where Republicans have a 53-47 majority.  

Republicans boast Senate super PAC, party committee edge

Despite Democrats’ advantage in key Senate contests, the Republicans’ top Senate super PAC is outraising its rival. 

The Senate Leadership Fund (SLF), affiliated with Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.), raised roughly $72 million in the first quarter, according to FEC filings, and ended with $166 million cash on hand.  

The Democratic counterpart, the Senate Majority PAC (SMP), brought in $56 million in the first quarter, ending with $75 million in the bank. 

SLF’s haul comes as the Republican-aligned group makes an aggressive push to flip Democrat-held seats in Michigan, Georgia and New Hampshire in the midterms, while defending GOP-held seats in more key states. Earlier this month, the group announced its largest-ever ad buy, a $342 million planned investment across eight Senate races.  

SMP pushed back against that announcement, calling it “a sign that Republicans are nervous.”  

Democrats’ House Majority PAC and House Majority Forward, though, outraised their GOP counterparts, bringing in a combined $69 million in the first quarter — while the Republican-aligned Congressional Leadership Fund and American Action Network raised $56.6 million. 

Meanwhile, President Trump’s MAGA Inc. super PAC has stockpiled more than $300 million, according to monthly filings from the end of February. The Republican National Committee also has a huge cash-on-hand advantage over their rivals, with $109 million in the bank compared to the Democratic National Committee’s $16 million, as of the most recent monthly totals.  

The Democrats’ Senate campaign arm had $30 million on hand at the end of February, while their Republican counterpart had roughly $33 million. The House arms for both major parties had roughly $57 million each.  

Big names spend big

Big-time donors on both sides of the aisle are pouring money into committee groups, outside groups and specific candidates, offering clues on how Republicans and Democrats are prioritizing their spending. 

Miriam Adelson donated $30 million to the Senate Leadership PAC, which backs GOP candidates, and another $10 million to the Congressional Leadership Fund backing House Republican candidates.  

Elon Musk, the tech billionaire and former Trump adviser, donated over $1 million to his America PAC. He’s weighed in on some midterm races this cycle, including in Kentucky where Republican Nate Morris is seeking to replace retiring Sen. Mitch McConnell (R).

He donated $10 million to a super PAC supporting the GOP businessman.  

Other Republicans backing Morris have included Zach Witkoff, the co-founder and CEO of World Liberty Financial and the son of Trump Middle East Envoy Steve Witkoff, who gave the pro-Morris super PAC $5,000. Claremont Institute chairman Thomas Klingenstein gave the super PAC $2.5 million.  

Former President George W. Bush donated $5,000 to Cornyn’s reelection bid in Texas, where he’s fighting for his political life in a GOP primary runoff against Paxton.  

On the other side of the aisle, billionaire George Soros funneled $50 million into his Democracy PAC via the Fund for Policy Reform. 

Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) has notably invested in races: Her campaign donated $4,000 to Peltola. Warren has also now given a total of $4,000 to Michigan Senate candidate Mallory McMorrow (D).  

James Murdoch, the youngest son of media magnate Rupert Murdoch, and his wife Kathryn Murdoch also donated $7,000 each to Peltola — $3,500 of which can be used in the primary and another $3,500 of which can be used in the general election.  

AI & crypto see mixed results on investments

The artificial intelligence (AI) and cryptocurrency industries have spent big in races up and down the ballot this cycle, with mixed results, particularly during the Illinois congressional races last month.  

Andreessen Horowitz was the top donor to one of the largest pro-artificial intelligence super PACs led by Silicon Valley leaders this quarter.  

The venture capital firm gave $25 million to Leading The Future, according to Wednesday filings. The super PAC is largely backed by venture capitalists Marc Andreessen and Ben Horowitz as well as OpenAI president and co-founder Greg Brockman and his wife Anna.  

Meanwhile, Cantor Fitzgerald donated $10 million to Fellowship PAC, a cryptocurrency super PAC. 

Fellowship PAC, which launched last year, reported $11 million in contributions in the first quarter of 2026. The remaining $1 million came from Anchor Labs, per the filings.  

Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick used to lead Cantor Fitzgerald. His sons Brandon and Kyle Lutnick now oversee the financial services firm.  

A separate filing Monday showed Fellowship PAC backed both Republican Senate candidate Nate Morris and Sen. Pete Ricketts (R-Neb.), spending $850,000 and $350,000 respectively. 

Julia Shapero and Sophia Vento contributed.

data.title: France calls on US to release 86-year-old widow of G.I. detained by ICE
data.description:
The French government on Thursday called on the U.S. to release an 86-year-old widow of an American G.I. in Department of Homeland Security (DHS) detention.

Rodolphe Sambou, consul general of France in New Orleans, told The Associated Press that his country’s government has “fully mobilized” to push for the release of Marie-Therese Ross, who has been in Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) custody since April 1.

“Given her age, we really want her to get out of this situation as soon as possible,” he said. “We want to get her out of jail.”

Sambou said he has met with Marie-Therese Ross twice while she has been detained and had been in contact with her family. He did not talk to the outlet about Ross’ legal status.

She is now being held at a federal immigration detention center in Louisiana, DHS told the AP. The department stated that Ross overstayed her 90-day visa.

The Hill has reached out to DHS for comment.

Marie-Therese Ross moved to the U.S. last year to be with Alabama resident and U.S. veteran William Ross. They met in the late 1950s while William Ross worked at a military base in western France, The New York Times reported. He married one of Marie-Therese Ross’ friends before he returned to the U.S.

Marie-Therese Ross and William Ross sparked a conversation after their spouses died. She moved to Anniston, Ala., and the two were married in April 2025, the Times wrote. Billy Ross died from natural causes on Jan. 24, according to his obituary.

Under state law, she would have inherited half of William Ross’ estate, the Times reported. His younger son Tony Ross testified to a probate judge that she did not want Billy Ross’ half, instead wishing for a way to return to France. William Ross’ sons offered to send Marie-Therese Ross $10,000 if she waived her right to the inheritance.

Calhoun County Probate Judge Shirley A. Millwood ordered the sons to not take or remove any of William Ross’ possessions after she claimed the sons rerouted her mail, causing her to miss an appointment on her immigration status, according to the outlet. Millwood on March 30 temporarily blocked any of William Ross’ relatives from removing any of his assets from his home.

Tony Ross, a retired Alabama state trooper who works at Millwood’s courthouse, testified that he did not order his colleagues to report Marie-Therese Ross. Millwood wrote in her ruling, however, that local police told Tony Ross the day before Marie-Therese Ross was be arrested that she would be detained and they texted him one hour before Marie-Therese Ross’ arrest.

Millwood is now seeking an investigation into the sons’ behavior.

data.title: Hawley pushes bill to bar lawmakers convicted of sexual abuse from receiving pensions
data.description:
Sen. Josh Hawley (R-Mo.) has introduced legislation to bar lawmakers convicted of sexual abuse from receiving federal pensions, a proposal that will get a lot of attention after former Rep. Eric Swalwell (D-Calif.) was accused of sexually assaulting a former staffer.

“Right now, a member of Congress can be convicted of sexual abuse and still receive a taxpayer-funded pension. That is unacceptable,” Hawley said in a statement Thursday afternoon.

“I’m introducing legislation to end this loophole and ensure that lawmakers are never compensated with taxpayer dollars after such a breach of trust. The only thing the government should be paying for is a jail cell for these people,” he said.

The No Pensions for Congressional Predators Act would prohibit members of Congress convicted of felony sex crimes from receiving their federal pensions.

Hawley says it would provide additional accountability in federal law for lawmakers who commit sex crimes.

“Right now, you could be convicted and still get your pension. The only thing that government ought to be paying for for people like Eric Swalwell is a jail cell,” Hawley said during an interview with Fox News’s Jesse Watters.  

GOP Rep. Lauren Boebert (Colo.) has called on Congress to revoke the pensions of Swalwell and Rep. Tony Gonzales (R-Texas), who both resigned from public office this week because of allegations of sexual misconduct.

Swalwell has denied the allegations of sexual assault.

Boebert said Swalwell and Gonzales should have been expelled and not allowed the more dignified exit of tendering their resignations.

“And I think that we actually need to look into ways to censure, with other aspects to say you can’t have your pension, you can’t leave here with all your taxpayer-funded benefits after such shameful acts that cause you to bow out and resign from Congress,” Boebert told CNN’s Manu Raju.

data.title: Bernie Sanders, labor leaders warn of AI risks for workers
data.description:
Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) and the leaders of several major labor unions warned Thursday about the risks AI poses to workers amid concerns about the technology’s ability to replace jobs.

Sanders, who is pushing for a moratorium on data center construction, argued that AI could displace both blue-collar and white-collar workers.

“How the hell do you go forward and throw millions of people out on the street without planning what’s going to happen?” the senator said at a press conference.

“Where do we want to go with the mission?” he continued. “Does AI and robotics have some positive things? Yeah. How do we use it to improve human life and not just make Mr. [Elon] Musk and his billionaire friends even richer?”

He was joined by AFL-CIO President Liz Shuler, United Auto Workers (UAW) President Shawn Fain, American Federation of Teachers (AFT) President Randi Weingarten and National Education Association (NEA) President Becky Pringle, among other union leaders.

“We are here to sound the alarms on AI and advanced technology because this is a worker’s issue, and this race that everybody seems to think we’re in to advance AI at all costs, with no guardrails or protections for people, is reckless and dangerous,” Shuler said.

The pushback comes as several AI leaders have suggested the technology could impact large swaths of workers.

Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei warned last year that as much as half of entry-level white-collar jobs could be eliminated by AI, while Microsoft AI CEO Mustafa Suleyman suggested in February that the technology could replace all white-collar work in 18 months.

Others, like OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, have forecast less dramatic scenarios for the workforce but still acknowledge that AI could disrupt the labor market.

Meanwhile, the push toward physical AI, like robotics, has spurred concerns about the fallout for blue-collar work.

Fain on Thursday compared the technology to the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA), pointing to what he described as the “false promises of shared prosperity” of the trade deal. NAFTA has been widely blamed for a decline in U.S. manufacturing jobs.

“The same greedy corporate power brokers today want us to believe that killing millions of jobs in the name of AI will be a good thing, and the working class knows better,” Fain said, adding, “We will not have our dignity robbed. We will not be made an adjunct to the tool of AI, and we will be heard.”

Both Pringle and Weingarten also took swipes at first lady Melania Trump over her recent appearance at a White House education event alongside a humanoid robot, which she suggested could eventually serve as an educator.

“Despite the false claims of the first lady, AI robots cannot replace human teachers,” Pringle said.

data.title: Trump picks former Deputy Surgeon General Erica Schwartz as new CDC nominee
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday announced Erica Schwartz, who served in a senior health role in his first administration, as his third pick to lead the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) more than six months after the last director was fired.

“It is my Honor to nominate the incredibly talented Dr. Erica Schwartz, MD, JD, MPH, as my Director of the CDC,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

“Erica graduated from Brown University for College and Medical School, and served a distinguished career as a Doctor of Medicine in the United States Military, the Greatest and Most Powerful Force in the World, and then served as my Deputy Surgeon General during my First Term. She is a STAR!” he added.

Trump additionally announced Sean Slovenski as his pick to be the CDC’s CEO, Jennifer Shuford to be the agency’s Chief Medical Officer and Sara Brenner to be Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s Senior Counselor for Public Health.

The role of CDC director has sat empty since Trump fired former director Susan Monarez less than a month after she was confirmed by the Senate. Monarez was the first CDC director to be Senate-confirmed after the new requirement was added.

Since her termination, the role has been occupied by acting directors Jim O’Neill and National Institutes of Health Director Jay Bhattacharya. The role has since exceeded the 210-day limit for a Senate-confirmed position to be vacant, leaving it unoccupied once again and vesting the majority of its function in Kennedy.
